skill.name: bioservices
skill.description: Use BioServices to query UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, PubChem, Reactome, QuickGO, and related bioinformatics services. The skill provides workflow guidance, reference tables, and example scripts for identifier mapping, protein analysis, pathway analysis, and compound lookup.
skill.summary: Query and connect major bioinformatics databases with BioServices workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review found that the high static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, biological database names, and terms such as Molecular Function. The confirmed risks are legitimate BioServices behavior: scripts contact external bioinformatics services and write user-selected output files. No prompt injection, obfuscation, command execution, credential theft, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed context.

medium_findings.title: External Bioinformatics Service Calls
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally uses BioServices clients that submit protein sequences, identifiers, compound names, and a BLAST email address to third-party scientific services. This is expected for the skill, but users should avoid sending sensitive or unpublished research data without checking the target service policy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs BioServices clients and submits BLAST jobs or identifier lookups. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: User-Selected Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several scripts write CSV, text, SIF, or JSON-style result files to paths chosen through command-line arguments or documented examples. This can overwrite local files if a user supplies an unsafe path, but no destructive deletion or hidden persistence behavior was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write calls are explicit and tied to user-specified output names or directories. The surrounding code shows report export behavior, not stealthy modification.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most external command, weak cryptography, C2 keyword, and dynamic function detections are caused by Markdown code fences, API examples, database abbreviations, or Gene Ontology terms. The reviewed locations do not show Ruby backtick execution, cryptographic use, command-and-control behavior, or JavaScript dynamic function construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is visible in Markdown examples or ordinary bioinformatics terminology. No executable Ruby, crypto API, or C2 logic appears in these reviewed contexts.

low_findings.title: Executable Workflow Scripts Require User Review
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill includes command-line scripts intended for local execution. They appear purpose-built for scientific workflows, but users should inspect arguments, output paths, and network submissions before running them on private data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts have clear command-line workflows and no hidden execution paths in reviewed sections. Review is still warranted because they combine network access with local output writes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Submission of User Biological Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed pattern: user-provided identifiers, sequences, compound names, and BLAST email values can be sent to external services through BioServices clients.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network submission calls are direct and expected for BioServices. The risk is data exposure to third-party APIs, not malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Writes to User-Controlled Output Locations
dangerous_patterns.description: Confirmed pattern: scripts open output files and directories based on command-line options or workflow parameters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The output path handling and file writes are explicit. This can overwrite files if used carelessly, but it is normal report export behavior.

content.value_statement: Researchers often need to connect protein, pathway, compound, and annotation databases across different identifier systems. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical BioServices workflows, scripts, and references for multi-database analysis.
actual_capabilities: Guides BioServices queries for UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, PubChem, Reactome, QuickGO, and related services.
Maps identifiers across protein, gene, pathway, compound, and ontology databases.
Provides complete scripts for protein analysis, pathway extraction, compound cross-reference, and batch ID conversion.
Explains workflow patterns for BLAST searches, GO annotations, PSICQUIC interactions, and pathway network exports.
Shows integration points for pandas, BioPython, NetworkX, and common scientific output formats.
limitations: Requires the Python bioservices package and working internet access to third-party databases.
Results depend on remote service availability, rate limits, and current database schemas.
BLAST workflows may require an email address and can take several minutes to complete.
The skill supports research workflows but does not validate clinical, regulatory, or medical conclusions.

use_cases.title: Map Protein Identifiers
use_cases.description: Convert UniProt accessions to KEGG, Ensembl, PDB, Reactome, or GO identifiers for downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Explore Pathway Networks
use_cases.description: Retrieve KEGG pathways, parse interactions, and export network files for genes or organisms.

use_cases.title: Cross-Reference Compounds
use_cases.description: Search compounds in KEGG and connect them to ChEBI and ChEMBL identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Protein Record
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioservices skill to show me how to retrieve the UniProt record and FASTA sequence for P43403.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Identifier Lists
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioservices skill to convert a list of UniProt accessions to KEGG and Ensembl IDs, then explain the output columns.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Pathway Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioservices skill to design a KEGG pathway analysis workflow for human genes and export interaction files.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Database Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the bioservices skill to create a protein workflow that combines UniProt search, BLAST, KEGG pathway discovery, PSICQUIC interactions, and QuickGO annotations.

output_examples.input: Find pathways for a UniProt protein and summarize the likely analysis outputs.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the UniProt entry, maps it to KEGG gene identifiers, lists matching pathway names, and describes exported pathway summary and interaction files.

output_examples.input: Cross-reference the compound Geldanamycin across databases.
output_examples.output: The response shows a KEGG compound match, related ChEBI information when available, a ChEMBL mapping through UniChem, and notes any missing links.

output_examples.input: Convert a batch of accessions from UniProt to Ensembl.
output_examples.output: The response explains the source IDs, target database code, successful mappings, failed mappings, and how to save the results for review.

best_practices: Check service terms before submitting unpublished sequences, compounds, or identifiers to public databases.
Use small batches, delays, and clear output directories when running scripts against remote APIs.
Record database versions, query dates, and unmapped identifiers for reproducible analysis.
anti_patterns: Do not treat remote database results as clinical evidence without expert validation.
Do not submit confidential sequences or private study identifiers to public services without approval.
Do not overwrite important files by reusing generic output names in shared directories.

faq.question: What is BioServices used for?
faq.answer: BioServices is a Python package for querying many biological web services through one interface.

faq.question: Which databases does this skill emphasize?
faq.answer: It focuses on UniProt, KEGG, ChEMBL, PubChem, Reactome, QuickGO, PSICQUIC, ChEBI, and related resources.

faq.question: Does the skill run analysis without internet access?
faq.answer: No. Most workflows require live access to third-party bioinformatics services.

faq.question: Can it process private or unpublished data?
faq.answer: Technically yes, but users should not send sensitive data to public services without policy approval.

faq.question: Does it include ready-to-run scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts cover protein analysis, pathway analysis, compound lookup, and batch identifier conversion.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk notice for external service calls and local file outputs.
